source_metadata.title_raw: Celeste Stahl Brady - A-19-017 - February 28, 2019 - Newport Beach

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Celeste Stahl Brady 
Stradling Y occa Carlson & Rauth 
A Professional Corporation 
660 Newport Center Drive, Suite 1600 
Newport Beach, CA 92660-6422 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-017 
Dear Ms. Brady: 
February 28, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the coriflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice 
QUESTION 
May two "Tenant Commissioners" of the City of Costa Mesa Housing Authority ("Housing 
Authority") take part in governmental decisions related to a planned project ("Project") to improve 
and operate City-owned affordable housing properties, given that they live in two affordable 
housing units? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The two Tenant Commissioners are legally required representatives of affordable 
housing tenants. Though certain governmental decisions pertaining to the planned project will have 
a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on the Tenant Commissioners' economic 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FileNo. A-19-017 
Page No. 2 
interests, that effect is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Thus, the Act does 
not prohibit the Tenant Commissioners from taking part in the governmental decisions. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
The Housing Authority owns and operates eight affordable housing properties ("Properties") 
improved with a total of 30 apartment units, all of which it acquired between 2013 and 2016. Under 
Section 34290 of the California Housing Authority Law ("HAL"), if members of the City Council 
also act as commissioners of the Housing Authority, and the Housing Authority owns property with 
tenants, then the Housing Authority Board must also appoint two of the tenants as "Tenant 
Commissioners." In June 2017, the City Council appointed Eloise Donaldson and Samira Reyes-
both of whom were tenants since before the Housing Authority acquired the Properties-as the 
Tenant Commissioners serving on the Housing Authority Board. Ms. Donaldson has lived in her 
apartment for at least 20 years and Ms. Reyes has been a tenant since 2015. The dates of original 
occupancy and tenancy by each of the Tenant Commissioners are not certain, but the records 
available indicate that Ms. Reyes signed a year-term lease in 2015, and Ms. Donaldson signed a 
year-term lease in 2014. 
Under existing regulatory agreements, the apartments are rented/leased to qualified, income-
eligible "low" and "very low" income households. Both Tenant Commissioners pay an "affordable 
rent" for their units under the applicable regulatory agreements of record. Ms; Reyes pays 
$813/month for her 2-bedroom "very low income" apartment and Ms. Donaldson pays $950/month 
for her 2-bedroom "low income" apartment. The market rate for comparable 2-bedroom units in the 
neighborhood is at least $1600/month. Due to limited documentation, it is uncertain if the Tenant 
Commissioners currently have yearlong leases, or if their prior year-long leases have converted to 
month-to-month tenancies. 
In October 2018, the Housing Authority and City jointly issued a request for proposals and 
qualifications ("RFP") seeking proposals from experienced developers and operators of affordable 
housing to improve and operate the Properties for continued long-term affordable housing. The RFP 
includes express statements that the selected developer, in its new Project and operation of the 
Properties, must allow existing qualified tenants (which would include the Tenant Commissioners) 
to remain in tenancy at an affordable rent of the lesser of affordable rent under the existing 
regulatory agreement or the affordable covenants of the new Project. The RFP proposals are due in 
late February and will first be reviewed by a panel of staff, consultants, and counsel. They will then 
recommend to the Housing Authority Board the selection of a developer, as well as authorize 
negotiation of an affordable housing agreement ("AHA") to implement the proposed Project. 
By continuing the RFP process, the Housing Authority Board will likely select a developer 
and development proposal, provide direction on the price and terms of the real estate transaction, 
and provide specifics about disposition and development of the Properties and undertaking of the 
Project that will be part of an AHA. Further, the RFP calls for the selected Project to include 
substantial rehabilitation, new construction or other capital improvement of the Properties, as well 
as an extended term of the affordability covenants for at least another 55 years. The extended 
covenant will allow the Tenant Commissioners (and all qualified tenants) to remain in tenancy at 
the Properties at an affordable rent so long as they continue to income-qualify and comply with 
tenant rules, leases and other covenants for occupancy at the Properties. 


ANALYSIS 
Interests 
File No. A-19-017 
Page No. 3 
Section 87100 of the Act provides, "[ n ]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest." A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or 
on an enumerated economic interest, including "[a]hy real property in which the public official has 
a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(b).) 
You have noted it is unclear whether the two Tenant Commissioners' original year-term 
leases have renewed on an annual basis or converted to month-to-month tenancies. This is a 
significant distinction that must be clarified in order to determine whether the Tenant 
Commissioners have an economic interest in the real property. 
Under Section 82033, an '" [i]nterest in real property' includes any leasehold, beneficial or 
ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction 
owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other filer, or his or her 
immediate family if the fair market value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." 
However, Regulation 18233 provides that "[t]he terms 'interest in real property' and 'leasehold 
interest' as used in Government Code Section 82033 shall not include the interest of a tenant in a 
periodic tenancy of one month or less." 
If the Tenant Commissioners have leases lasting longer than one month, and each Tenant 
Commissioner has at least a $2,000 interest or more in her respective lease, then each commissioner 
has a potentially disqualifying leasehold interest in real property. However, if the Tenant 
Commissioners' previously year-long leases have converted into month-to-month leases, they do 
not have potentially disqualifying leasehold interests in real property under the Act. 
Additionally, each '_fenant Commissioner has an economic interest in her respective personal 
finances. (Section 87103; Regulation 18702.5.) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial effect on an economic interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
economic interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official 
or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Based on the facts in this case, under Regulation 
18702.2, the reasorn1bly foreseeable financial effects of a governmental decision on any real 
property in which a governmental official has a leasehold interest is material whenever the 
governmental decision will: 
• 
Change the termination date of the lease; 
• 
Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; or 


• 
Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property. 
FileNo. A-19-017 
Page No. 4 
If each commissioner has a leasehold interest in Housing Authority's real property, the 
governmental decisions at issue would have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on 
those interests. As stated above, the RFP calls for substantial rehabilitation, new construction or 
other capital improvement of the Properties, as well as an extended term of the affordability 
covenants for at least another 55 years, which will allow the Tenant Commissioners (all qualified 
tenants) to remain in tenancy at an affordable rent so long as they continue to income-qualify and 
comply with tenant rules, leases and other covenants for occupancy. Although the extension of the 
affordability covenants would not technically change the termination date of the leases, it would 
have a similar effect, as it would allow the commissioners to remain in affordable housing for at 
least another 55 years (as long as they continue to qualify). At the very least, the Project would 
increase the potential rental value of the Properties and impact the officials' use and enjoyment as a 
result of the planned improvements. Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions 
relating to the Project will have a material financial effect on any leasehold interest the Tenant 
Commissioners may have. 
Alternatively, Regulation 18702.5(a) provides that a financial effect on a public official's 
personal finances is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive a 
measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision.2 If the Tenant Commissioners merely have 
month-to-month leases, then they will still receive the measurable financial benefits of subsidized 
housing each month that they choose to rent their apartments. Tenant Commissioner Reyes pays 
$813 per month for her "very low income" apartment, while Ms. Donaldson pays $950 per month 
for her "low income" apartment. The market value for comparable units is at least $1600 per month, 
a savings of several hundred dollars each month and thousands of dollars each year. As noted, 
governmental decisions pertaining to the Project and RFP would not only improve the properties 
themselves, but may also allow the Tenant Commissioners to continue living at the properties for 
the foreseeable future. Thus, even if the Tenant Commissioners' formerly year-long leases have 
converted to month-to-month tenancies, the governmental decisions pertaining to the Project will 
still have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on their personal finances. 
Public Generally Exception 
Although the governmental decisions pertaining to the Project would have reasonably 
foreseeable, material financial effects on the Tenant Commissioners' economic interests, the Act 
only prohibits a public official from partaking in a decision if the decision's effect on the official's 
interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Commonly 
referred to as the "public generally exception." Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the general rule: 
A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is 
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a 
significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial 
interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment. 
2 Regulation 18702.S(c) provides, however, that "[i]fthe governmental decision affects a business entity or real 
property in which the official has a financial interest, this regulation does not apply and materiality is determined under 
Regulation 18702.1 or 18702.2." 


FileNo. A-19-017 
Page No. 5 
Regulation 18703(e) lists specific rules for special circumstances, in which the financial effect 
on a public official's financial interest is expressly deemed indistinguishable from that of the public 
generally. This includes when the governmental decision at issue "is made by a board or 
commission and the law that establishes the board or commission requires certain appointees have a 
representative interest in a particular industry, trade, or profession or other identified interest, and 
the public official is an appointed member representing that interest." (Regulation 18703(e)(5).) 
The rule "applies only if the effect is on the industry, trade, or profession or other identified interest 
represented and there is no unique effect on the official's interest." (Ibid) 
You have explained that the Tenant Commissioners have been appointed as a requirement of 
the California Housing Authority Law, given that the City Council also serves as the Housing 
Authority Board, and the Housing Authority owns property with tenants. In other words, the Tenant 
Commissioners are required, appointed members representing the interests of tenants of the 
Housing Authority. The reasonably foreseeable, material financial effects of governmental 
decisions related to the Project are not unique to the Tenant Commissioners; rather, the effects 
would be felt by all tenants of the Housing Authority, who would similarly benefit from 
improvements to the Housing Authority properties, as well as the sustained provision of affordable 
housing. Accordingly, then, the "public generally exception" pertaining to required representative 
interests (under Regulation 18703(e)(5)) applies. The Tenant Commissioners do not have 
disqualifying financial interests and they may take part in governmental decisions related to the 
Project. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division
